primary_diagnosis.name: Extragenital lichen sclerosus (mild) with secondary health anxiety

pathophysiology_summary: Lichen sclerosus is a chronic autoimmune dermatosis producing porcelain-white, atrophic, wrinkled ('cigarette-paper') plaques; the extragenital form (trunk/shoulders) is often asymptomatic and slowly progressive. It is benign and not necrotic or infectious. Suddenly discovering the pale, shiny plaque prompted fear of skin necrosis or a spreading infection, and vigorous rubbing produced a reactive (blanching) erythematous ring that reinforced the fear of spread. The differentials to exclude — morphea (deeply indurated, bound-down), tinea corporis (active scaling border, itch), and cutaneous necrosis/vasculitis (pain, dark eschar, ulceration, poor capillary refill) — are all absent, and the reassuring features (painless, superficial atrophy, blanching rub-erythema, no necrosis/ulcer/pus) are visible over video.

differential.evidence_basis: Painless porcelain-white superficially atrophic plaque with cigarette-paper surface.
Distinguished by deep induration/bound-down skin; this is superficially atrophic.
Screen for pain, dark eschar, ulceration, poor capillary refill; absent here.
No active scaling border or itch.
Painless, non-necrotic, non-ulcerated (DI-001) porcelain-white superficially atrophic plaque with only rub-induced blanching erythema (DI-002) — benign extragenital lichen sclerosus.
No pain, eschar, ulceration or perfusion compromise — necrosis/vasculitis excluded.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: reassure benign, stop the rubbing, gentle emollient, routine GP/dermatology confirmation (± topical steroid), and a necrosis/infection safety-net. ED escalation for a painless white plaque is over-triage.

red_flags.description: Necrosis/infection — pain, dark eschar, ulceration, spreading warmth/pus, or fever
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed necrotising process could progress.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Escalating a painless white plaque with rub-induced blanching redness to ED for presumed necrosis/flesh-eating infection.
Not distinguishing superficial lichen sclerosus from deeply indurated morphea (which can restrict movement) — a reason for in-person confirmation.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of induration/depth and bound-down assessment
Skin biopsy if diagnosis is uncertain
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Reassure benign; stop rubbing; emollient; routine GP/dermatology confirmation; necrosis/infection safety-net.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Over-triaging a benign plaque wastes resources and amplifies anxiety; a genuine necrotic/vasculitic lesion would need urgent care (hence retained safety-net).
